claim: Autologous transplantation of mesenchymal stem cells has higher rates of rejection than induction therapy with anti-interleukin-2 receptor antibodies.

result.claim: Autologous transplantation of mesenchymal stem cells has higher rates of rejection than induction therapy with anti-interleukin-2 receptor antibodies.
result.reasoning: The claim states that autologous transplantation of mesenchymal stem cells has higher rates of rejection than induction therapy with anti-interleukin-2 receptor antibodies. However, evidence from a randomized controlled trial (Paper ID: 22436957) directly refutes this. This study, with a sample size of 159 human participants, found that the use of autologous mesenchymal stem cells resulted in a lower incidence of acute rejection (7.5% and 7.7% in MSC groups) compared to anti-IL-2 receptor antibody induction therapy (21.6% in the control group). This evidence is strong and directly contradicts the claim.

evidence.text: None of the patients in either autologous MSC group had glucorticoid-resistant rejection.
evidence.source: Induction therapy with autologous mesenchymal stem cells in living-related kidney transplants: a randomized controlled trial.

evidence.text: 4 of 53 patients (7.5%) in the autologous MSC plus standard-dose CNI group had biopsy-confirmed acute rejection.
evidence.source: Induction therapy with autologous mesenchymal stem cells in living-related kidney transplants: a randomized controlled trial.

evidence.text: The study compared autologous MSCs and anti-IL-2 receptor antibody induction therapy in renal transplant patients.
